summary:
The early phase of the Poverty Point cultural tradition refers to the beginnings of the Lower Mississippi Valley mound-building complex that would culminate in the impressive earthwork site of Poverty Point in northeastern Louisiana, dated in its mature form to approximately 1700–1100 BCE. The roots of the tradition, however, extend back to the Middle Archaic period — represented by the nearby [[event:1597|Watson Brake]] mound complex — demonstrating that monumental earthwork construction in this region had a history stretching back several thousand years before the better-known Poverty Point culture.

The early Poverty Point cultural tradition encompasses hunter-gatherer communities of the lower Mississippi Valley who subsisted on the exceptional aquatic and terrestrial resources of the region. Long before they constructed the major earthwork complexes associated with the culture, communities were developing the social networks, exchange systems, and organizational capacities that would eventually support large-scale monument building. Poverty Point culture is particularly noted for its extensive exchange networks, through which objects including stone from distant sources were acquired and distributed across the lower Mississippi Valley and beyond.

The Poverty Point site itself — a massive earthwork complex with concentric semi-circular ridges covering over 900 acres — was built later (c. 1700–1100 BCE) but represents the culmination of a long local tradition of mound building and community organization. The early Poverty Point period documents the social antecedents of this later florescence, demonstrating the deep roots of complex society in North America's southeastern woodlands.
